title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Best Focaccia Genovese Recipe with Rosemary & Olive Oil
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of Focaccia Genovese with our authentic recipe. Learn to bake this classic Italian bread, infused with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil. Perfect for any meal!
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary and Olive Oil
h2Headings: Pro Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese
seo.imageAlt: Freshly baked Focaccia Genovese with fragrant rosemary and glistening olive oil, ready to serve.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a slice of Italy with this irresistible Focaccia Genovese, topped with aromatic rosemary and rich olive oil. Perfect for dipping!

description: Immerse yourself in the rustic charm of Liguria with this authentic Focaccia Genovese. Golden, airy, and exquisitely savory, each bite reveals the fragrant essence of fresh rosemary and the rich depth of extra virgin olive oil. Perfect as an appetizer, side, or simply enjoyed on its own.

ingredients.notes: plus more for oiling and drizzling

instructions.title: Activate Yeast and Combine Wet Ingredients
instructions.text: In a large bowl, whisk together the warm water and active dry yeast. Let it sit for 5 minutes until foamy. Stir in 2 tablespoons (30ml) of extra virgin olive oil.

instructions.title: Mix and Knead the Dough
instructions.text: Add the flour and fine sea salt to the wet ingredients. Mix with a spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Transfer the dough to a lightly floured surface and knead for 10-12 minutes until it is smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky.

instructions.title: First Rise (Bulk Fermentation)
instructions.text: Lightly oil a clean bowl with olive oil. Place the dough in the bowl, turning it once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let it rise in a warm place for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until doubled in size.

instructions.title: Shape and Prepare for Second Rise
instructions.text: Generously oil a 9x13 inch baking pan with olive oil. Gently transfer the risen dough to the pan, being careful not to deflate it. Gently stretch the dough to fill the pan. Cover again and let it rise for another 30-45 minutes.

instructions.title: Dimple and Top the Focaccia
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 220C (425F). Use your fingertips to gently create deep dimples all over the surface of the dough. Drizzle generously with more extra virgin olive oil, sprinkle with the chopped fresh rosemary, and finish with a pinch of flaky sea salt.

instructions.title: Bake to Golden Perfection
instructions.text: Place the focaccia in the preheated oven and bake for 25-35 minutes, or until golden brown on top and cooked through. The bottom should also be golden. Remove from the oven, let cool slightly in the pan, then transfer to a wire rack to cool completely before slicing.

tips: Use good quality extra virgin olive oil; it makes a significant difference in flavor for Focaccia Genovese.
Do not skip the rising times. Proper fermentation develops the flavor and texture of the focaccia.
For extra crispiness, bake the focaccia directly on a preheated baking steel or pizza stone, if available.
article:
Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil: A Taste of Ligurian Tradition

Imagine the sun-drenched coast of Liguria, Italy, where the air is fragrant with sea salt and rosemary, and the aroma of freshly baked bread wafts from every panificio. At the heart of this culinary landscape lies Focaccia Genovese, a masterpiece of Italian baking that transcends simple bread. It’s a symphony of texture and flavor: crisp on the bottom, impossibly tender and airy within, adorned with fragrant rosemary and glistening with the finest olive oil. This isn't just any focaccia; it's a testament to centuries of tradition, a beloved staple that perfectly embodies the simplicity and richness of Italian cuisine. If you're searching for the ultimate Focaccia Genovese recipe, one that captures the true essence of Genoa, you've come to the right place. Get ready to embark on a delicious journey that will transform your kitchen into a Ligurian bakery.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?

Focaccia Genovese, or *Fügassa* in the local Ligurian dialect, is more than just flatbread; it's a cultural icon. Originating from Genoa, the capital of Liguria, this focaccia stands apart from its counterparts across Italy. Its defining characteristics are its thinness, especially at the base, which achieves a delightful crispness, contrasting beautifully with its incredibly soft, airy, and chewy interior. Unlike thicker, more pizza-like focaccias, the Genovese version is typically less adorned, allowing the quality of its core ingredients – flour, water, yeast, salt, and crucially, copious amounts of extra virgin olive oil – to shine.

The distinctive dimples on its surface aren't just for aesthetics; they serve a vital purpose. These indentations catch pools of olive oil and a brine made of water and salt, which seeps into the dough during baking, contributing to its unique flavor and texture. The result is a bread that is remarkably moist, rich, and flavorful, with a golden-brown crust and an irresistible sheen. Finished with a generous sprinkle of coarse sea salt and fresh rosemary, Focaccia Genovese is a versatile bread, enjoyed at any time of day – for breakfast with coffee, as a snack, or as an accompaniment to a meal. Understanding the essence of this bread is key to mastering the Focaccia Genovese recipe.

## Ingredients You'll Need

Creating an authentic Focaccia Genovese doesn't require a long list of exotic ingredients, but the quality of each component is paramount. For the best results, always opt for high-quality extra virgin olive oil and fresh yeast.

*   **For the Dough:**
    *   500g (about 4 cups) strong bread flour (Type 00 or all-purpose flour with high protein content)
    *   350ml (about 1 ½ cups) lukewarm water
    *   10g (about 2 teaspoons) fine sea salt
    *   5g (about 1 ½ teaspoons) active dry yeast or 15g fresh yeast
    *   10g (about 2 teaspoons) sugar (feeds the yeast)
    *   40ml (about 3 tablespoons) extra virgin olive oil, plus more for the pan and drizzle

*   **For the Brine and Topping:**
    *   50ml (about ¼ cup) water
    *   10ml (about 2 teaspoons) extra virgin olive oil
    *   5g (about 1 teaspoon) fine sea salt
    *   Fresh rosemary sprigs, leaves stripped
    *   Coarse sea salt, for sprinkling

Let's dive into the essential components of our Focaccia Genovese recipe.

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Patience is a virtue when baking focaccia, as the long fermentation process is key to its characteristic flavor and texture.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a small bowl, combine the lukewarm water with the sugar and yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** In a large mixing bowl, combine the flour and fine sea salt. Pour in the activated yeast mixture and 40ml of extra virgin olive oil. Mix with a spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms.
3.  **Knead (or Stretch and Fold):**
    *   **Traditional Kneading:** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 10-15 minutes until it is smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky.
    *   **Stretch and Fold Method (Recommended for higher hydration doughs):** Leave the dough in the bowl. Every 20-30 minutes for about 2 hours, moisten your hands and gently stretch a portion of the dough up from the side of the bowl, then fold it over onto itself. Rotate the bowl and repeat this 3-4 times. This develops gluten without intensive kneading.
4.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Lightly grease the bowl with olive oil. Place the dough in the bowl, turn it once to coat, then cover with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let it rise in a warm place for 1.5-2 hours, or until doubled in size. For a more complex flavor, you can cold proof the dough in the refrigerator overnight (8-12 hours) after the stretch and fold stage.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Generously grease a 9x13 inch (or similar size, roughly 23x33 cm) baking pan with extra virgin olive oil. Don't be shy; this oil contributes to the crispy bottom crust.
6.  **Transfer and Dimple:** Gently transfer the risen dough to the prepared pan. Pour another tablespoon or two of olive oil over the top of the dough. Using your oiled fingertips, gently stretch and press the dough to fill the pan, creating deep dimples all over the surface. Take care not to deflate it completely.
7.  **Second Rise:** Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let the focaccia rise again for 30-45 minutes in a warm place, or until visibly puffy.
8.  **Prepare the Brine and Top:** While the focaccia is undergoing its second rise, prepare the brine by whisking together 50ml water, 10ml olive oil, and 5g fine sea salt. Once the focaccia has risen, gently pour the brine evenly over the dimpled surface. Press the fresh rosemary leaves into the dimples and sprinkle generously with coarse sea salt.
9.  **Bake:** Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F). Place the focaccia in the preheated oven and bake for 20-25 minutes, or until deeply golden brown on top and crispy on the bottom.
10. **Finishing Touch:** As soon as it comes out of the oven, drizzle a little more extra virgin olive oil over the hot focaccia for extra sheen and flavor.
11. **Cool and Serve:** Let the focaccia cool slightly in the pan for 5-10 minutes before transferring it to a wire rack. Slice and serve warm.

## Pro Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese

Achieving that perfect Ligurian focaccia involves a few key secrets that elevate a good Focaccia Genovese recipe to an extraordinary one.

*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** This is arguably the most important ingredient. Invest in a high-quality, fruity extra virgin olive oil. It contributes significantly to the flavor, aroma, and texture. Don't be afraid to use it generously in the dough, in the pan, in the brine, and as a final drizzle.
*   **High Hydration Dough:** Authentic Focaccia Genovese has a very wet, sticky dough. This high hydration is what gives it its characteristic open, airy crumb. Resist the urge to add too much flour during mixing or shaping. Oiling your hands helps manage the stickiness.
*   **Long Fermentation (Cold Proofing):** While a warm rise works, a long, slow fermentation in the refrigerator (8-12 hours or even up to 24 hours) develops a much deeper, more complex flavor and better texture. Plan ahead for this step!
*   **Deep Dimples:** Don't be shy when creating those dimples! Press your fingers down firmly, almost to the bottom of the pan. These pockets are crucial for holding the olive oil and brine, creating those irresistible pockets of flavor and preventing the dough from puffing up too much like a regular bread.
*   **The Brine:** The water and salt brine is a signature of Focaccia Genovese. It seeps into the dough, seasoning it from within, keeping it moist, and contributing to the crispy crust.
*   **Preheat Your Pan (Optional but Recommended):** For an extra crispy bottom, place your empty baking pan in the oven as it preheats. Carefully remove it, grease it, and then add your dough. The initial burst of heat helps create a superb crust.
*   **Don't Overbake:** Focaccia should be golden brown, not dark and dry. Keep an eye on it to ensure it remains moist and tender inside.

These insights will elevate your Focaccia Genovese recipe from good to extraordinary.

## Serving Suggestions

Once you've perfected your Focaccia Genovese recipe, the possibilities for enjoying it are endless.

*   **As a Snack:** The simplest and perhaps most traditional way to enjoy Focaccia Genovese is plain, perhaps with a cup of coffee for breakfast, or as a mid-afternoon snack. Its rich flavor needs no embellishment.
*   **With Charcuterie and Cheese:** Serve warm slices alongside a selection of Italian cured meats like prosciutto or salami, and some aged cheeses such as Parmigiano Reggiano or Pecorino.
*   **Dipping:** It’s excellent for dipping into high-quality extra virgin olive oil, perhaps with a splash of balsamic vinegar, or even a flavorful pesto.
*   **Soup and Salad Companion:** Focaccia is a wonderful accompaniment to hearty Italian soups like minestrone or a fresh, vibrant salad.
*   **Sandwiches:** Slice it horizontally to create unique, flavorful sandwiches. Fill with mozzarella, tomatoes, basil, and a drizzle of balsamic glaze, or with grilled vegetables and goat cheese.
*   **Appetizer:** Cut into small squares or strips and serve as an appetizer at your next gathering.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the classic Focaccia Genovese recipe is perfect in its simplicity, you can certainly experiment with variations to suit your taste.

*   **Toppings:** Before the second rise or just before baking, you can add other toppings. Popular choices include:
    *   **Cherry Tomatoes:** Halved cherry tomatoes pressed into the dimples.
    *   **Olives:** Pitted Kalamata or Ligurian Taggiasca olives.
    *   **Onions:** Thinly sliced red or white onions, sometimes caramelized slightly first.
    *   **Herbs:** Thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs can be used instead of or in addition to rosemary.
*   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of finely grated Parmigiano Reggiano or Pecorino Romano can be added after the focaccia comes out of the oven, or even baked on for a crispier cheese topping.
*   **Spicy Kick:** A pinch of red pepper flakes can be added to the dough or sprinkled on top with the coarse salt.
*   **Flour Blends:** While strong bread flour is traditional, you can experiment with a blend, perhaps replacing a small portion with whole wheat flour for a nuttier flavor, though this will affect the texture slightly.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Creating a gluten-free Focaccia Genovese recipe is more challenging, as gluten is key to its airy texture. However, various gluten-free flour blends specifically designed for bread making can be used, though the results will differ from the traditional version.

Armed with this comprehensive Focaccia Genovese recipe, you're now ready to bring the authentic flavors of Liguria into your home. The process is rewarding, and the result – a tender, aromatic, and utterly delicious focaccia – is well worth the effort. Enjoy the journey of baking this iconic Italian bread!
